segments.text: for the Māori Affairs Select Committee. We've got representatives from across Parliament here,
eager and ready to discuss with you the annual review for the period ending June 2024.
It's only a few months ago, I think, that we've seen this for the previous years in your review.
So thank you for coming in and for your work at Fikata Māori. We've only got 45 minutes
with you, so maybe I'll leave it open to yourselves for a brief opening remarks, maybe three minutes,
and then we're just keen to have a good quarter with you today. So over to you.
O i mihi ane koutou, tēnāi wāngā o te tā, wātua reua kiterangi, mātiti ki te whenua.
Nōrera, kō tāi mai ahine raumati, kō te makarere tonu ki Ponekenei.
O i mihi ane koutou, kē aku mua ngāpurangatira.
Vision and Mission, kia mauri ora tereo, which is still the vision and mission for the organisation
as we move forward, and our sort of compass to work by in terms of the outcomes that we're
seeking to achieve. But I thought I'd just sort of touch on hātai whetū, which is really the
operational framework that we've been focused on as an organisation, and it has, you know,
this has been inspired by mahu tunga, you know, the Southern. We've got to look to the
stars for guidance. But really what it does is it just sort of reminds us of, you know,
we want to be very much a kōpapa Māori focused organisation, so we are drawing on
some of our own wisdom to guide the organisation moving forward, but it also
supports the organisation from a governance right down to the operational level
around how we implement and execute our roadmap.
So, when we think about that, the three core principles are the revitalisation of our
ill and how we embed that in every aspect of our work and what we do, the impact that
we haven't have on our audience and so we'll share some of the highlights for the year
and review with you in terms of our progress in that respect but ultimately how we can
improve the engagement that we have with our audience and really the role we play
and sort of really bringing that pride and confidence among, the cultural pride
and confidence among our whānau and our audience and then the big thing for us
is around sustainability and I think we'll spend a little bit of time touching
on Hawaiwhakata which has been the realignment process that we've been
through which really has focused on ensuring that one yes we are fit for
purpose but that we are financially sustainable moving on, moving into the
future and you're aware of the reduction in funding and some of the key
areas of focus that Shane and the team have had around the structure of the
organisation but also importantly thinking about you know what are the
levers that we control, we don't control the ministers unfortunately
around funding, we can influence but really it's around cost control, the
limited opportunities there are to generate third party revenue but
really thinking about the limited resource we have, how do we get the
greatest impact from that to deliver to our audiences and that really
sort of comes back to Huipai, Tātaiwhetu and our framework and how we
measure our performance against that. The sort of third slide really
sort of reflects on some of our key achievements for the year and I'll
get Shane to sort of touch on some of these but you know the audience
engagement in reach, I mean I think we're very proud of our team so
as a board and I think as our kai urungi, our executive management you
know a lot of the results that we are seeing is a consequence of the
dedication and commitment of our...
of our kaivoi. So, you know, our 2.5 million viewers from our television audience, our digital platforms continue to grow.
I don't know if you might have seen one of our recent announcements on our TikTok post, was really down to one of the members on the committee here.
So, what are we at now?
Nearly 400 million.
400 million views relating to a particular sort of act in Parliament.
But that's interesting because that's...
Everything helps.
Because it's about getting that reach you go.
Actually, you know, from a whakata Māori standpoint, it actually does, you know, we come in front of mind,
not only in our domestic audience, but also globally in terms of the role we play more broadly among Indigenous broadcasters.
So, our digital platforms engagement, you know, about 4.8 million users, and we continue to sort of build on that.
We'll talk about this, you know, as we said last time, a lot of the realignment process that we've been through has been to sort of transition the organisation to be much more...
to be digital first and digitally led.
Because, as you know, that's where many of our audience lives now and so how we can respond to that.
And, you know, we've had sort of good stats around how we've contributed to our audiences increasing their deal,
which is, you know, over 50% of 57%.
And the immersion piece, too, has sort of grown.
Probably the only other point I'll touch on is the impact that we're having on community.
So, you know, we talk about te reo me ngā tikanga, and so te reo was one component,
but also, as I said, when we think about the role we play in sort of inspiring the broader population
and audience to have pride in our culture and our sort of ways of being and knowing.
So, you know, as a consequence of some of our work, our impact, 78% of Māori viewers felt more connected with their ideas.
identity through our programs and so on, which I think that's a good sign and how we're encouraging
and inspiring people to use it all among their whānau. One of the big sub-areas, which is
a focus for us just because of the demographic make-up, is that focus on the Rangatahi segment,
which is interesting because Rangatahi and I don't think Shane and I sort of characterise
ourselves in that sort of, but very different. And so I think one of the, I'd say it is more of an
opportunity around media organisations like ourselves, which is all part of that realignment
process, is how do we ensure that we are and we continue to be relevant to that Rangatahi
segment because their consumption of content and the type of content is very different.
And then you've got the overlay of where does the real content fit within that mix as
well on those, through those channels and those platforms that they are living on.
So we've got a slide for, I might sort of just ask Shane to talk about the digital sort of
transformation process because I think that's really been the key area of focus for our
organisation and what we want to do moving forward. And I think just acknowledging the
committee in terms of, you know, we've talked a number of times about the fact that we're
20 years old, we've still got the same piece of legislation. So I think, you know, getting
some movement on amendments to our piece of legislation to ensure that the settings that
we operate within our sort of fit for purpose as well is really important, you know,
because, you know, we're not only television, we're going to be living in that digital
space that are moving forward. So I'll just invite Shane to make some comments on
the digital transformation process.
Kia ora tata. Just two points, really. I suppose the digital transition began four
years ago, so that's a journey that we continue to embark on.
When you see significant year on year growth,
it tells us that the approach and plan is working.
We have, as Jamie has said, just recently completed the realignment process
and that has now created a new structure
with new roles that will continue to support that digital shift.
We're looking forward to seeing the fruits off that recent change
Te Omari-Nzib said it had had a pay breakfast owners.
Palaka tila we hardest doing about culturally changing.
If we look, just with the net Państwo offer,
that platform alone must bring a huge impact to our program
to be recognized and respected by the media.
Ma Herbert Hazard iwi, ma nanu i pakbopi kai te reiri.
Anubatrote.
There Mediterranean txand para pa i Vega.
Parada kuetau he ma嘛 te re 미ch часa.
Şa móai ma syrando ka?
Ma
kura kawek i kevēicting
Mā fus courte g….
Ma zotlenan maini ki
wedi eispabaon

angola ʻo

I think Shane you know when we look across that review and realignment process I mean it affected probably 95% of our staff in some way so I think you know I want to just acknowledge that
challenges that that's had on our kai mahi, but certainly looking forward to next year to
serve looking at what we can do with our new new align structure.
Kia ora.
All right, well thank you Jamie and Mr Taurima. We now go to questions, so who has questions?
Shannon Helmett.
Firstly, can I just acknowledge you both for the incredible mahi
that you and your team do, far beyond the expectation and on the smell of an oily rag
in many respects, but I'll cut to the chase, which is really around that reduction in funding
over the past year. That does concern me significantly and I've watched very closely the impact on
staff, many of our friends in whānau of course, and I just wonder in terms of is this it,
or do you anticipate that there'll be additional cuts to your staffing as a result of your funding?
You get first to know.
Sure, based on our current funding projections, this is it, but of course that will all depend
on any future decisions that may be made by government around broadcasting and media
priorities, but at this stage what has been executed and decided is how we will proceed moving
forward. One point I would just make is that while the Waifakata redeployment process,
realignment process sorry, saw a net reduction of 27 FTE roles, we have over the year
been applying a sinking lid policy and so there are many more roles that have been
disestablished as a result of our imminent funding loss. So we're looking at about a quarter
of our staff workforce that have been lost as a result of our imminent funding loss.
result of the imminent funding changes.
So 27 humans and staffing resource,
what's lost in terms of your delivery,
I know, a shift to—
There's 44 roles in total.
So there were some unfilled roles,
but 27 directly impacted.
I mean, part of it, it's really sort of approaching our whole—
we've got a totally different business model
and operational model now
around that digital first sort of strategy,
which requires us to think very differently
at an operational level around how we make,
curate and actually provide content to audiences
through those sort of new digital platforms.
I think previously we talked about
some of the transmission costs
around, you know, the second te reo channel.
And as you may have read, I mean,
we're looking to turn that te reo channel off in March next year.
And so our te reo channel will be—
and all of that content will be solely online.
And so how does that work with trying to achieve
our number of speakers in the country
or our te reo Māori strategy?
What's lost?
What's at risk?
So the decision for the transition of te reo
to online only, there will be some impact there,
especially with some of our older,
rarally-based viewers.
However, that's why we made that announcement
when we did to prepare and to help communicate the change
that will take place in March next year.
And we need to acknowledge that there are some
within that demographic that solely rely on
linear television.
The good thing, though,
is that we will still maintain the other te reo channel
and we will still uphold our commitment
to reo Māori programming on that channel.

So, while there is a loss of one TV channel, we will still have one and we also look to the significant growth that we've seen in digital to further support our role and ability to deliver to ensure that not only are we fulfilling our statutory obligations but also our funding requirements as well.
What additional revenue streams are you investigating what's on the table? We can government help to unlock some of those opportunities.
So, as part of the Waifikaata process, we engaged with our staff as well as the wider sector and public to understand the opportunities that we have to be able to generate new revenue.

And we gathered a range of different ideas and we've got a number of ideas that we still have to work our way through that has been put on hold while we undertook the realignment process and that's something that we're looking to further explore next year.

Can we ask us, can I ask us up on that? One of the major buckets of revenue streams like are we looking at the organizations that we're looking at, you know, how do you, you know, add revenue onto digital?

How are you going to try and increase that, you know, keen to understand, understand it's on hold in your canvas, but your leadership team must have a pretty good view on what is the business model of the future look like

and how do you transition from where you are now to there? Well, this is the challenge that all media agencies face, right? And you only have to look as far as TVNZ and Warner Brothers Discovery to see just how tough it is out there in the market.
Currently, our advertising revenue is only reflected in about 1% of total revenue. So, that's the starting base, if I could put it like that.
But there are opportunities that we are looking to further explore.
investment but you know we still need to engage in good faith in those discussions
and it's also I think understanding what other indigenous models there are globally. So as
an example our counterparts at NITV in Australia the government introduced a great initiative over
there where government advertising comms budget they have to give at least 3% of that to NITV
is what they call a 3% initiative and that has seen incredible investment go into NITV
which has been needed for some time. That is an example I suppose of a model that is working
and that could be achieved with government support. Right thank you. Further questions?
Just looking at your priority outcomes and noting some really great improvements and increases
and I just have a curiosity around viewer increase in understanding of Te Reo Māori
through content which has decreased from 67 to 63% and noting that there's likely a correlation
between that and the swelling numbers of viewerships globally. You know noting that for example
there's an increase in understanding of Māori perspectives in tikanga from non-Māori
viewers. So my question really is how do you maintain a balance when you're casting a wider
net and having a growing audience of continuing to build Te Reo Māori knowledge and efficacy
while engaging and broad audience who may be engaging for the first time with Māori content.
So that's quite a difficult balance to strike and then something entry to the
that is how do you do that with the shrinking budget?
So just on the first part of the question, it is a fine balancing act and that's
why that thinking takes place when we're developing and designing our
investment plan and our approach to commissioning new content.
One point that I would just add is that there was a delay in our budget
being confirmed in the period under review.
So our final budget wasn't actually confirmed until the second
quarter. Now that had a flow on impact or effect on decisions
that we could make about content.
And so what I would suggest to you is that that has had an
impact on some of the results.
It was pleasing to see actually most actually saw growth.
However, that particular result that you talk about didn't.
And there's no doubt because, you know, content is queen, content
is king. And when you don't have the content, then you will
see a flow on effect from that.
There's probably an additional point to which, you know, in the
broader ecosystem, our role versus the role of other
organisations within the sector, and it's something that
when we come together, thinking about how we each
contribute to those sort of objectives and aspirations and
what really is going to be our key focus, which I think, you
know, the development of Te Huapai, thinking about
where we have a unique role and what we can sort of focus
on. And I think reminding a lot of people that, you know, we
are in everyone's homes, we have the ability to be in
everyone's homes and say, I think there's a broader
conversation here around the sector as a whole, the
tereo sector and the allocation of resourcing to
achieve what.

pateculu outcomes, because you are right, you know, if you are resourced, you have a greater ability to target a particular segment, but at the same time, how do you weigh that up in terms of those competing tensions and objectives?
Kia ora, so I just have another question to follow up on that, noting, and I'm sure others will probably want to ask about similar things, noting that incredible uptake in online media engagement
especially the 1000% increase in viewership of social media content. That swelling seems to me unsustainable, but maybe I'm wrong as you reach into global markets more.

So how do we sustain and maintain consistent growth that may not look like that, but continues to proliferate te reo Māori content, especially online, when there's budget restraints. So I'm just interested to know what the strategy with that is.

If you think about some of the outcomes that we're seeking to achieve, we want to see not only the number of viewers engaged in our content, but actually the difference or the impact that that content is making on them.

And we think there's richer insights from that informational data as opposed to 1000% increase, as an example. And so when you think about some of the KPIs that we have about understanding how content has impacted a viewer and whether or not it has increased their use of te reo, their understanding of te reo, their connection to Māori culture, and that's for Māori and non-Māori.
That's when you really start to see the value and impact of whakata Māori. So I think that that's what we will be continuing to monitor and reflect in the content decisions that we are making.
when we announced earlier this year, for example, about less funding going into content,
but high-impact content.
We need to ensure that we're not just investing in content that are going to get as many eyeballs
as possible, but actually content that is also going to move the needle in terms of
language revitalization because both are measures that are really important to the
work that we do.

So on-going monitoring of those, making sure that we're reflecting those insights and data in the decisions that we're making, whether we have a lot of funding or shrunk funding as we know we have moving forward.
So, I mean, what we wanted, and we were getting better at it, aren't we Shane, in terms of, you know, I mean, I think I've used the analogy in select committees in the past.
I mean, if our audiences want steak and we're only serving up sausages, and so the thing is, if we want to serve up steak, it does require us to focus.

And I think, you know, strategically at a governance and operational level, I think we've just realised that, you know, we've got to sort of, we're probably going to only, we can only afford to develop less content,
but it's got to be much more impactful to deliver on the outcomes that we want.

But I think as we, as we sort of start to execute and implement the new strategy, content strategy, as Shane says, you know, are we getting good, deep insights to understand the impact that that content is having?
And what are we learning from that? What might we do differently moving forward? So, you know, we're wanting to be much more data driven.
But, you know, how do we collect data? How do we utilise it? How do we sort of gain clean those insights?
And so that's really the focus. And I think, you know, one of the benefits of being digital first, you are able to collect a lot more data than we have from sort of linear television.
And so, you know, through that process, we're going to have to leverage partners to support us in that journey as well.
But it's certainly going to be the focus.
��를 diero. Kaipi turu angatiau, ma greeting i rai Breata, beti i kai rai teisakao, hwni tu nama mela
ma grei, ba diraunai teisakao.
Imele, n railroadite.
Ano, te angoni i teisakao, poko nga itai i teisakao, i teisakao i teisakao i teisakao, i teisakao i teisakao
i teisakao i teisakao i teisakao i teisakao, i teisakao i teisakao i teisakao, i teisakao i teisakao i teisakao i teisakao, uzi
And that's the dual role that we hold, right?
And I think that's important to remember,
because we are the only media,
national media organisation that has that dual role
as media and broadcasters,
but also to support language revitalisation.
And I think that's, yeah,
an important reminder for us all.
I just have one final follow-up on that
speaking around content decisions and I see
one of the documents have shared with us.
You speak about whānau-raising children in Te Reo Māori.
You speak about arangatahi audiences, active Te Reo Māori learners.
And I have a particular interest in education, Māori education specifically.
And I just want to know if you can point me or reference
what Māori educational strategies or philosophies
kind of are being utilised in this.
Is it Te āpumatua? Have you developed your own?
And what informs kind of the uphaul aspect of your content?
So, most of our educational programming that we currently deliver
was produced and commissioned many years ago.
So, you don't have to look very far to still see,
call it or my, with Pytipi Taylor, who looks 18 years old.
And that's been a direct result of the shrinking funding
that we've had, where we've had to prioritise, right?
The great thing about educational programming
is that, for a learner, it's still a programme that is relevant for them,
today as it is, in 10 years' time.
But that's been a direct...
konsequence of the funding decisions that we've had to make.
And saying that, we see that there's a real opportunity
to partner and collaborate with MOE,
with Ministry of Education,
and we've done some of that in previous years.
As an example, during the global pandemic,
we partnered with MOE to deliver
total immersion instruction language programming
right throughout that period of time,
and that was really successful.
So we're still looking at further collaboration
with the Ministry around how we can further support,
noting that, yes, while active language learners
are a key target audience for us,
we're not educators,
and so that's why we look to partner
and collaborate with educators
so that we can support the work that they do
through the medium and the content that we deliver.
I think that's it, that point around understanding
where we sit within the broader system, too.
I think we can all be better at that, the system,
in terms of understanding,
having almost like the single outcomes framework
and about understanding how each of the sub-organisations,
both within government and externally,
contribute to the revitalisation of real
across those different learners.
We've just got a sub from David McLeod.
Jamie, just on that point there,
I mean, there are many other entities
that are in a similar space, you know,
around te reo, and all that sort of stuff.
So how are you working with those other entities,
with a common vision, a common journey itself?
So what's your interaction with other entities?
Yeah, well, we've, I mean,
we meet often, we talk often about te whare o te reo maori ora
and whare maihi and our respective roles in that.
We've had some particular views around the imbalance of funding
and making sure you get the right balance
between where investment is made for te reo.
taron going. I think, you know, we have had six years of review. I think it's one of the points we've made previously.
And I think we're getting to the pointy end of all of this to say we need to get to a point where
the reviews are complete. Decisions are made and we're all very clear around, you know, how we are all aligned and working towards
the ultimate objective around te reo me ngā te kanga say. I think, you know, we're involved in a number of conversations at present
which will hopefully lead to some decisions being made, sort of mid next year, around the broader future, around a lot of the reo entities.
And particularly, you know, the role that we can continue to play as whakata Māori to support that.
I mean, I'm recently confident that the Te Huapai and Tātaiwhetu, so our outcomes framework and our operational strategy that we have
and support and deliver on the broader outcomes, but we need to ensure that the broader ecosystem is sort of aligned.
Do you think there's advantage in being the autonomy of the organisations or potentially there's actually potential benefit from being closer together?
There's different sort of perspectives on this, I think. I mean, I'm a big believer in ensuring that the system

incentivises the right behaviour and the right outcomes. I think the challenge that I'll set that has had since my time on the board of whakata Māori is that, you know, the consequence of under-investment
and limited funding courses cannibalisation. And so, you know, people won't naturally sort of come together when you're fighting for your own survival.

So I think, you know, over the past period we've sort of been trying to get over that particular sort of challenge and get to a place where, and I know this because all of the chairs that our entities are fully committed,
we often sort of say that, you know, a lot of us could be doing some other things, but we do what we do.
I know our kai hoi and our kaimai i pakaata Māori do what they do because they're very passionate about the kaupapa and te reo.
But I think we need to solve this collectively to find a way forward where we've got a sustainable model
and I think we've got to be mindful that many say well to be sustainable it's about cost cutting and reducing entities and consolidation
but I think we've got to get to a place where we can strategically think about the way forward structurally from a fiscal financial stand point
but ultimately does it fit for purpose to deliver the outcomes we want.
Now whether that's autonomous or I think we've just got to work through that.
There's been a number of recommendations in the past six years.
I mean we're very hopeful and confident I think with the right leadership collectively
that's both with government and the sector that we can find a way forward
and we have to look at the challenges we face.
We're pretty confident that what we've done around the realignment pool over the next five years
subject to any other decisions but it's not sustainable long term within the next 10, 15, 20 years
so I think we've got to be mindful of that and we're coming from the context of we're 20 years old
and we've still got the same legislation.
So you know again it's so where is the urgency to sort of ensure that we're able to evolve with time as well.
The only point that I would just add to that is that when you think about the entities that we have
that support language revitalisation there is an opportunity for greater alignment
because all entities have been established at a time as a consequence of time
and if you take a step back there is a real opportunity I think to achieve real alignment
in the language revitalisation space.
I think on a day-to-day basis there's a...
real genuine intent and desire to collaborate and partner and work together and so we see
many fine examples of that. However, something more coordinated, I think, would, if that's going
to achieve better real outcomes, would be a good way moving forward. We've got a supplementary
You've just mentioned the legislation needing to be modernised. What are the key things that
you would like to see there? Well, the primary one is that we exist to provide linear television.
I think some of the reporting requirements, I mean, you know, we were born at a time
where I guess there was not the level of trust and confidence in establishing institutions like
Fokata Māori, so I think, you know, certainly the board's perspective is that there is still a
level of paternalism within our legislation that should no longer be required. I think, you know,
if you look at our organisation, you know, governance and operational systems, processes,
I mean, another clean audit, you know, that I think there's a level of confidence and capability
within not only our organisation, but the sector, which I think we can start thinking about,
you know, what is the right level of oversight from whether it's government and funders and the
like. So I think that's that's one piece around the accountability levels. And then I think just
really reflecting what the feature is. And the challenge, of course, as you know, with,
you know, statutory entities is that, you know, you've got to have the right level
of imbalance of prescription within the legislation, but also be mindful of
ensuring there is sufficient flexibility to be able to operate in an ever-changing,
evolving world, so. And inovate. And inovate. So I think, you know, these are some big sort
of questions. There's some immediate things that we've already developed up.
athin, on the back of this select committee recommendations to look at this, I mean we've
really got some reports that can be provided, but I think our first principles level, the
big thing around any changes is, you know, how do you balance the level of accountability
required given we are sort of publicly funded and then sort of ensuring you've got the
flexibility to be able to innovate and change.
Has the Minister made any commitments around this?
We've had discussions with the Minister over a number of meetings around some of the
challenges and working quite, you know, working actively with the Minister around
what we can do over the next few months.
Does that include privatising a kātamādon?
Haven't had that discussion around privatisation, but I think there's probably a broader
is a broader conversation to understand whether it's ownership or whether it's
who an institution like Pākata Māori should be accountable to.
If we think about our marae o ireangi, I know with Te Kori Makau, you know,
with OFM, you know, Te Kori Makau and Taranaki are zoned by our iwi, yes,
there is funding from Te Māngai Pāhu and others, but a lot of our ireangi
throughout the moutū are actually sort of funded by iwi as well.
So I think we've, certainly our perspective is that we've got to be open-minded
around what the possibilities are, what the potential structures are moving forward.
Because ultimately, you know, we want institutions that are going to have the
flexibility but be able to deliver on the mission and the objective.
Kia ora.
Next we go to Hannah Rafferty.
Might be Clark.
Thank you, Mr Chair.
Tena kōrua.
Kia ora.
Kia kōrua me o koutou kai mahi me ngā Ōwatanga o te wa.
Mai mahi rau i te wa, i te amariki anau i te wa.
Reira a tupakata Māori ki New Market.
Ngāi rewae au ki te raha i ngā waka pa.
Ngā reira nui ngā Ō…
erti ngā maharakirera.
I have a few questions.
The first one is around me and Benjamin looking at the data,
increasing 55% non-Maori engaging with your content.
I was just wondering if there is an appetite for Fikata Māori
in the global market with the rising demands of Te Reo Māori content
and Te Ao Māori content.
We asked the same pātai with Aote Mā Ngai Paahau
Te Ao Pēwhairangi going over to the Olympics
and the great, I guess, social media
being able to be like a doorway for international viewers.
So is there an appetite for that
and how would you be able to keep up with that
with the stagnant funding?
And then my second pātai is around transforming,
moving over to the digitisation through social media platforms.
And this is a bit of a weird question,
but if you receive any funding from those apps also,
knowing that there's also challenging times with TikTok,
Instagram and the legislation to place in the US
is the kind of plans around that also.
On the second question, we don't currently.
And one of the key reasons for that
is because we're also considered to be a news provider as well.

Just more on the engagement piece for me.
What's a cost?

to serve in it. Are there any differences in the cost to serve for linear TV versus social media like you deliver a product or TV and then it just goes, is it different? I mean, we're not really in the industry. So fill us in like what is quite a huge differential for, you know, on the production side?

So from a delivery perspective, there is significant difference. So, for example, when you deliver content via TV, via linear transmission methods, it's a one-off cost, basically. And that has no bearing on how many people are engaged in that content.
So it could be 10 million people. It's a one-off cost, fixed cost. In terms of content for digital, the more people that engage with your content, the more expensive it becomes.

And that's actually been one of the challenges that we've had because we've been primarily funded for linear. Well, we've only been funded for linear. And so what that has meant is that, and as you've seen through some of the figures that we've shared,
there is a downside to the success because it has become more expensive. So if I think about...
Explain what becomes more expensive.
So, and this isn't the production of the content, but basically the more people that engage in the content, the more costly it becomes. And so as a result...
Wait, sorry.
Sorry.
In what way?
It's to do with the...
I'm going to try and explain this in a simple way. If you think about the space that it takes to be able to hold the content...
So, the more people that engage and use the content, the more costly it becomes because of the greatest space that you require to hold that content.
It's the bean bro and the data space.
So it's Hamlin's fault.
It's a difference.
So when you think about it, just in terms of delivery costs, I think it was around 125% increase for us in the period under review.
And I can check that for that.
But it was something pretty significant.
Yeah.
In terms of production costs, because again, how much does it cost to make the programme?
It depends on the type of content.
So as an example, digital news, where you're not having to fully rely on the big cameras, et cetera, in the gear and the equipment,
and where you could potentially train a multi-media journalist to be self-sufficient does help with cost.
And so therefore it becomes a little bit less expensive to create.
But again, it comes back to the type of content.
Overall, though, for bigger types of content, drama, et cetera, it's pretty much the same.
No, thank you.

Te Reza, I've just hesitated on this. I didn't want to go back over territory that the committee had already explored.
But if time permits, could we just go back again to the organisation of the four Māori language entities.
Encouraged to hear you guys talk about Te Whakātumāori, te whakarau ora o te reo mewana tikanga, te whakarau o te reo.
But if that's so, if that's the primary aim of Whakātumāori, then I'd be just keen to hear you guys speak more to what the future looks like in terms of the relationship between those four entities.
And if I can go here to what the arrangement of those four entities might look like in terms of their respect or their maybe combined relationship with government.
And just what I'm thinking about there is Te Reo Māori is a taonga.
And again, my apologies if this ground's already been covered.
Yeah, come in briefly, I think. As we said earlier, you know, i kānau maia nei ohu i to rātau wā.
So each we're born at a particular time within a particular context.
And I think there is opportunity to sort of think about how we should sort of align ourselves but also structure ourselves in the future.
So I think the pleasing thing is their active conversations that have been going on for some time.
But I think becoming much much more urgent because of the challenges that each of us face around funding and also just thinking about how we can.

and deliver more together, and so, as I say, those are active conversations and we're hoping that probably mid-year there would be at least some options and potentially some decisions around what the future of this sector might be.
I think too, it's about recognising that each entity has its own whakapapa, and so if there was any potential change, how do we do that in a way that still acknowledges the whakapapa of each entity?
Because while te reo he taonga, te mahi a tēnā tēnā he taonga ano, and I think that we're very supportive of any initiative that can help achieve better reo outcomes.

Recognising that for us, as an example, we have a unique role. So while we're here to support language revitalisation, we do that through content, and we have that reach and platforms to be able to do that.
So that's a unique role that we fulfil. But in saying that, we also commission and fund content as well. So there's potential opportunities, I think, there for realignment across that space.

If we're thinking about the difference between if there was the indigenous approach that Australia had with the government spending on advertising being directed versus the digital stuff.
